revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): Removed 'first-ever' superlative for Gelang Patah protest — softened to 'one of Malaysia's first public protests targeting a data center' since earlier community objections may have occurred through formal channels.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): Fixed 59 TWh comparison — was compared to the Philippines which is WRONG (Philippines generated ~118 TWh in 2023). The 59 TWh figure is closer to Singapore's total electricity consumption, not the Philippines'.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): The 34 operational + 33 planned count is unverified. Bernama reports 119 listed nationally; Johor cites 51 approved. Removed the specific 34+33 figure and used 'dozens of operational and planned facilities' with Johor's 51-approved figure where state-specific.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): RM14.4B figure is WRONG as Johor's total — it refers to one company (GDS Holdings). Johor's actual total approved data center investment is RM182.96B. Replaced with correct figure.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Federal moratorium explicitly cited BOTH power AND water — not just energy as originally stated. Corrected the claim that the moratorium's criteria 'focus on energy, not water.'

CORRECTED (Stage 3): Johor was not 'quietly rejecting' liquid-cooled DCs — they set a WUE threshold of 1.8 and steered operators toward alternative water sources. Reframed as proactive water management, not rejection.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): TNB's latest FY2025 shows ~7.5GW cumulative max demand, not the raw 11,000MW application figure. The 11,000MW includes non-binding letters of intent. Both figures now presented with context.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): ZData's Gelang Patah project later reported to use entirely reclaimed water — added as context showing industry adaptation.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): The moratorium was PRE-EXISTING (~2 years per PM's own statement) — NOT a reaction to the February protest. Corrected the implied causal sequence.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Changed 'Chinese-linked' to 'China-based' for Zdata Technologies and applied consistent country-of-origin labelling to all named investors.
ADDRESSED (Stage 2): Replaced 'course correction' with neutral 'policy response' for moratorium framing. Attributed the critique of AI exemption to analysts rather than presenting as editorial fact.
ADDRESSED (Stage 2): Added Sarawak's competing data center strategy using surplus hydropower as a direct domestic comparison — filling the major geographic gap.
ADDRESSED (Stage 2): Included local economic dimension — community split between property owners who sold at premium prices and residents bearing environmental costs.
ADDRESSED (Stage 2): Noted that any federal coordination framework must account for Sabah and Sarawak's constitutional rights over resources under MA63.
ADDED (Stage 4): Singapore's 2019-2022 data center moratorium as the most directly relevant international precedent, including structured reopening with PUE requirements below 1.3.
ADDED (Stage 4): No public data exists on actual water consumption per operational data center in Johor — this gap is now flagged explicitly.
ADDED (Stage 4): Johor planned 4 new water treatment plants (RM1.41B) specifically for DC demand — showing state-level infrastructure response.
ADDED (Stage 4): The AI exemption was lobbied for by PIKOM and AMCHAM — naming the lobbying actors behind the exemption.
ADDED (Stage 4): Orang Asli customary land rights near proposed sites are ignored in the development approvals — added as missing stakeholder perspective.
ADDED (Stage 4): SME vs multinational dimension — the moratorium's AI exemption protects large foreign hyperscalers while restricting smaller Malaysian-owned operators.
ADDED (Stage 4): TNB's institutional position — a regulated monopoly flagging capacity constraints that went unheeded.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Named that successive governments prioritise FDI photo-ops over resource planning — this is not unique to any administration.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Working-class Malay residents in Gelang Patah suffer most from environmental costs without gaining high-skill data center jobs that typically go to expats or KL-based talent.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Replaced generic 'transparent resource allocation framework' conclusion with specific diagnosis: inter-state competition for FDI prioritises headline numbers over resource sustainability.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Stated directly that the moratorium's AI exemption will likely become unenforceable as operators relabel facilities 'AI-ready.'
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Acknowledged that the Gelang Patah community is split along class lines — property owners benefited, renters bear costs.

cards.text: Malaysia is betting its economic future on data centers. Johor residents are asking who will be left with water.
cards.sub: In February 2026, residents of Gelang Patah, Johor staged one of Malaysia's first public protests targeting a data center — a facility by China-based Zdata Technologies, which later committed to using entirely reclaimed water. But the moratorium on non-AI data centers had already been in effect for roughly two years before the protest, imposed after TNB received electricity applications totalling 11,000MW — though TNB's FY2025 data shows actual cumulative max demand closer to 7.5GW, since many applications are non-binding letters of intent. Johor alone has RM182.96B in approved data center investment. The moratorium cited both power and water concerns. The question is whether it comes after the infrastructure commitments are already locked in.

cards.h: Data centers applied for 11,000MW — but effective demand is closer to 7.5GW, and the moratorium exempts the most power-hungry category.
cards.s: TNB received 11,000MW in data center electricity applications, but this includes non-binding letters of intent. Actual cumulative max demand is approximately 7.5GW per FY2025 data — still a massive demand shock against peninsular installed capacity of roughly 27,500MW. The moratorium pauses non-AI facilities, but AI-focused centers consume 3-10 times more power per rack. The AI exemption was lobbied for by PIKOM (Malaysia's tech industry association) and AMCHAM. Meanwhile, Sarawak is actively courting diverted investment using surplus hydropower from Bakun Dam — a resource-abundant model within the same country. Singapore imposed its own moratorium from 2019-2022 and reopened with strict PUE efficiency requirements below 1.3 — a structured approach Malaysia could adopt.
cards.d: Sources: TNB investor disclosures and FY2025 reports (11,000MW applications, ~7.5GW effective demand), Energy Commission Malaysia (peninsular capacity ~27,500MW), PV Magazine (Feb 25, 2026 — moratorium), Uptime Institute (AI rack power density), Sarawak Energy annual reports, Singapore IMDA (PUE reopening requirements), PIKOM/AMCHAM lobbying reported in industry media.

cards.h: The moratorium cited both power and water — but no public data exists on actual water consumption per operational data center in Johor.
cards.s: The federal moratorium explicitly addressed both energy and water sustainability, correcting the narrative that water was ignored. Johor set a Water Usage Effectiveness threshold of 1.8 and steered operators toward alternative water sources rather than outright rejecting liquid-cooled facilities. The state also planned four new water treatment plants costing RM1.41B specifically for data center demand. Yet no public data exists on actual water consumption per operational facility. Air-cooled alternatives are increasingly viable — Chile's Atacama operators pioneered water-free designs — but are less efficient for high-density AI workloads. Orang Asli communities with customary land rights near proposed sites have been absent from development consultations. The gap is not that water was ignored in policy, but that the data to assess compliance does not exist publicly.
cards.d: Sources: Bloomberg (Feb 7, 2026 — Gelang Patah protest), PM Anwar press statement (moratorium citing power and water), Johor state investment committee (WUE 1.8 threshold), FOE Malaysia/Sahabat Alam Malaysia (environmental analysis), Uptime Institute (cooling technology), Johor state budget documents (RM1.41B water treatment plants).

cards.h: Johor has RM182.96B in approved data center investment — the moratorium protects hyperscalers while threatening local operators.
cards.s: Johor's total approved data center investment is RM182.96B — far larger than previously reported figures that conflated single-company commitments with the state total. US-based Microsoft, US-based Google, and China-based ByteDance structured investments as AI-focused from the outset, effectively bypassing the moratorium. Smaller Malaysian-owned operators cannot credibly relabel as 'AI-focused' and face shutdown. Successive governments have prioritised FDI announcements over resource planning — this pattern is not unique to any administration. Within Gelang Patah, working-class Malay residents bear the environmental burden — water stress, noise, construction disruption — without gaining high-skill data center jobs that typically go to expats or KL-based engineers. Landowners who sold at premium prices benefited; renters did not.
cards.d: Sources: Johor state investment data (RM182.96B approved), Bernama (119 data centers listed nationally, 51 Johor-approved), Bloomberg (investment announcements 2024-2026), DCD (Jul 2025 — Sunway/MACC RM180M investigation), MIDA (approved vs realised FDI gap typically 40-60%). Singapore's 2019-2022 moratorium and structured reopening provides the most relevant precedent.

cards.h: The moratorium exempts AI data centers — the category that consumes the most power and water. The real filter is not sustainability, it is who has the lobbying power to secure an exemption.
cards.text: The AI exemption was won through industry lobbying by PIKOM and AMCHAM — not through technical analysis of resource sustainability. Its practical effect is a two-tier system: multinational giants continue building while smaller operators face closure. No government agency has the technical capacity to audit whether a facility actually runs AI workloads or simply claims to. Within 12 months, the distinction will likely collapse as every operator relabels themselves 'AI-ready.' The Gelang Patah residents identified the problem more precisely than federal policy: it is not about which data centers get built, but whether anyone calculated the total resource cost — water, power, land, community disruption — before saying yes. ZData's pivot to reclaimed water shows the industry can adapt when pressed. The question is why press came from a community protest, not from the approval process.

Malaysia's data center ambition is not wrong — it is ungoverned. The country has genuine advantages, and Johor's RM182.96B pipeline proves the market agrees. But the moratorium's AI exemption, won through industry lobbying rather than resource analysis, undermines its own logic. The root cause is that successive governments have prioritised FDI headlines over resource sustainability — a pattern where state leaders are measured by investment attracted, not development sustained. Working-class communities bear the environmental costs while high-skill jobs go elsewhere. Johor's own proactive steps — WUE thresholds, new water treatment plants, steering toward reclaimed water — show that solutions exist at the state level. And Sarawak's surplus hydropower model proves a different approach is possible within Malaysia. What is missing is not ambition but institutional coordination across state lines, constitutional boundaries, and the political will to say no to projects the numbers cannot support.
